seo.title: Max CPA Explained for Ecommerce: Formula & Worked Example
seo.description: Max CPA is the highest cost per purchase you can afford before ads lose money. Learn the formula, ecommerce example, and how it connects to ROAS and CPC.

headline: Max CPA Explained for Ecommerce
intro: Max CPA is your break-even cost per purchase — the highest CPA Google or Meta can deliver before you lose money on each order after product costs.

sections.heading: What max CPA means for ecommerce

blocks.text: CPA (cost per acquisition) is ad spend divided by purchases. Max CPA equals contribution margin per order — profit per sale before ad costs. Spend $45 to acquire a customer when max CPA is $39 and you lose $6 per order regardless of revenue ROAS.
Max CPA = (AOV × margin%) − fixed cost per order
AOV $90, margin 50%, fixed costs $6 per order.

sections.heading: How max CPA connects to ROAS

blocks.text: Max CPA and break-even ROAS describe the same economics differently. ROAS compares revenue to spend; max CPA is the absolute dollar cap per conversion. Both derive from contribution margin.

faq.question: What is max CPA in ecommerce?
faq.answer: Max CPA is the highest cost per purchase you can pay and still break even. It equals contribution margin per order before ad spend.

faq.question: How is max CPA different from target CPA?
faq.answer: Max CPA is your ceiling. Target CPA in ad platforms is what you bid toward — often set below max CPA to preserve profit margin.

faq.question: Does max CPA include shipping costs?
faq.answer: Fixed per-order costs like shipping and payment fees reduce contribution and therefore reduce max CPA. Include them in the calculation.
